coredata.dc:title: Stimuli-responsive luminescent nanomaterial based on lanthanide modified carbon dots for multilevel information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lina
Zhang, Feng
Chen, Junyu
Jia, Lei
Li, Yongxin
Xu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Multistimuli responsive luminescence material have tremendous potential in various advanced photonic applications, especially in high-level information encryption. Here, a facile and effective way was presented to construct multistimuli intelligent luminescent nanomaterial based on europium modified carbon dots (CDs-Eu) for advanced information encryption. This nanocomposite exhibiting controllable response behaviors upon exposure to acid-base, 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) and Cu2+ hold an enormous potential in multilevel encryption application. The fluorescence (FL) color of CDs-Eu can be reversibly switched between colorless and green upon acid-base stimulation. Furthermore, in the presence of the deciphering agent (DPA), the fluorescence color of CDs-Eu exhibited a green to red color change, which can be restored upon another external stimulus (Cu2+). Combined with Morse code and anti-counterfeiting pattern, the encryption, decryption and anti-counterfeiting of important information can be realized. This stimuli-responsive luminescence material with high confidentiality shows great potential in advanced information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stimuli-responsive material
Carbon dots
Multilevel encryption
Anti-counterfeiting
Lanthanide

Response mechanism of CDs-Eu to pH, DPA and subsequent Cu2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and size distribution images of (a) CDs and (b) CDs-Eu; (c) EDS spectrum of CDs-Eu; (d) XPS of the CDs-Eu.
The high-resolution XPS of C 1s (a), N 1s (b), O 1s (c) and Eu 3d (d) in CDs-Eu.
(a) FL spectra, (b) digital photo, (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates and (d) reversible response behavior of the CDs-Eu in Tris−HCl buffer solutions with different pH.

(a) Changes in FL intensity of the CDs-Eu in the presence of DPA, (b) the CIE chromaticity diagram of the CD-Eu after adding DPA, (c) the change of the FL intensity of the CDs-Eu-DPA after adding Cu2+, (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of the CD-Eu-DPA after adding Cu2+ .
Demonstration of reversible encryption of the word “COMPUTER” represented by Morse codes using CD-Eu fluorescent ink.
Display of multilevel anti-counterfeiting of patterns of bear paw (a), footprint (b), bird (c) and toxic hazard sign (d).
Structure and anti-counterfeiting schematic of CDs-Eu nanosensor.

head.ce:title: Stimuli-responsive luminescent nanomaterial based on lanthanide modified carbon dots for multilevel information encryption

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft
Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation
Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition
Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Henan Polytechnic University, #2001 Shiji Street, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multistimuli responsive luminescence material have tremendous potential in various advanced photonic applications, especially in high-level information encryption. Here, a facile and effective way was presented to construct multistimuli intelligent luminescent nanomaterial based on europium modified carbon dots (CDs-Eu) for advanced information encryption. This nanocomposite exhibiting controllable response behaviors upon exposure to acid-base, 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) and Cu2+ hold an enormous potential in multilevel encryption application. The fluorescence (FL) color of CDs-Eu can be reversibly switched between colorless and green upon acid-base stimulation. Furthermore, in the presence of the deciphering agent (DPA), the fluorescence color of CDs-Eu exhibited a green to red color change, which can be restored upon another external stimulus (Cu2+). Combined with Morse code and anti-counterfeiting pattern, the encryption, decryption and anti-counterfeiting of important information can be realized. This stimuli-responsive luminescence material with high confidentiality shows great potential in advanced information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security plays an important role in economy, military and human life. The development of advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption materials has become the focus of researchers' attention [1–4]. Among them, fluorescent materials are commonly used as anti-counterfeiting materials due to their advantages of simple identification means, rich detection signals and identification with the naked eye [5]. However, fluorescent materials with static luminescence confront many challenges in the practical application, because most of them have the disadvantages of low complexity and high predictability, which makes the encrypted information easily cracked [6]. Stimulus-responsive fluorescent materials show great application potential in information storage, security and anti-counterfeiting due to their controllable optical properties (absorption, emission intensity and wavelength, luminous life, etc.) under the external stimuli (such as light, electricity, force, heat, ions, etc.) [7–9].

So far, many stimulus-responsive luminescent materials have been explored, such as organic dyes [10], complexes, carbon dots [11,12], quantum dots [13], etc. It is worth noting that for luminescent materials with absorption in the visible range, no matter how the light is illuminated, the pattern is easily identified under sunlight, which affected the information encryption process [14]. On account of this, luminescent lanthanide (Ln) materials are especially attractive as they have been extensively investigated for information security because of small absorption cross section of Ln ions under sunlight [15,16]. Among them, Ln doped/modified anti-counterfeiting materials have attracted extensive attention due to their advantages of rich fluorescence (FL) color, adjustable luminous life, high luminous efficiency and low toxicity, etc [17]. Stimulus response lanthanide anti-counterfeiting materials have become a research hotspot in recent years [18]. However, most of them demonstrate single-stimulus responses, which have certain limitations in improving the information security [19]. Therefore, it is urgent to explore multistimuli response fluorescent materials to meet the requirements of high security anti-counterfeiting and information storage. The combination of the stimulus response of materials with the fluorescence properties of Ln is the key to the application of Ln elements for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. Scheme 1.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a typical luminescence material that can absorb various excitation light and convert it into visible light emission. It also has many advantages involving controllable luminescent color [20], outstanding light stability, low toxicity and biocompatibility [21]. Thus, the exploration of CDs-based security technology is conducive to the establishment of advanced and secure information encryption [22,23].

Based on the above considerations, a multistimuli response intelligent luminescent nanomaterial based on europium modified carbon dots (CDs-Eu) was elaborately tailored for advanced information encryption [24,25]. This CDs-Eu nanocomposite exhibited controllable color changes upon exposure to acid-base, 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) and Cu2+, respectively. The fluorescence color of CDs-Eu can be reversibly switched between colorless and green through acid-base stimulation. Furthermore, in the presence of the deciphering agent (DPA), the fluorescence color of CDs-Eu exhibited a green to red color change, which can be restored upon application of another external stimulus (Cu2+). Combined with Morse code and anti-counterfeiting pattern, the encryption, decryption and anti-counterfeiting of important information can be realized [26,27]. This multistimuli responsive luminescence anti-counterfeiting nanomaterial with high confidentiality shows great potential in advanced information transmission.

Pamolic acid (98%) was purchased from Saen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Urea (99%) was from Hongyan Test Plant in Hedong District of Tianjin, and europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.99%) was purchased from Shanghai Energy and Chemical Industry. NaOH was purchased from Tianjin Continental Chemical Reagent Factory, HCl was purchased from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Triethylamine (TEA, 99%) was purchased from Shandong West Asia Chemical Co., Ltd., and 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA, 99%) was obtained from China Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. CuCl2 was obtained fromTianjin Kaixin Chemical Industry Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were recorded on a Tecnai-G2-F30 microscope at 300 kV acceleration voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the material was studied by using the standard KBr disk technology and Bruker V70 infrared spectrometer in the wave number range of 4000 – 400 cm−1. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum was measured by Lambda 950 spectrophotometer of Perkinelmer Medical Diagnostic Products Shanghai Co., Ltd. On the Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer, the luminescence spectra of the nanomaterials were collected at 280 nm excitation wavelength.

Green fluorescent CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Brifely, 116.5 mg pamolic acid and 18 mg urea were dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water, and ultrasonically dispersed uniformly. Then the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Bring to room temperature before centrifuging (9500 rpm/min) to get rid of the precipitate. After filtering through a filter membrane (0.22 µm), the supernatant was dialyzed against distilled water for 2 days by using a cellulose membrane (MWCO 1000). The powder of CDs was collected by vacuum freeze-drying.

50 mg of CDs were dispersed in 50 mL distilled water, After that, 50 mg of Eu(NO3)3 was added into the above mixture. Stirring was continued for 6 h at 60 °C. The resulting solution was dialyzed against a cellulose membrane (MWCO 1000) for 48 h. The product was collected by freeze-drying the dialysate. The coordination between Eu3+ ions and carboxyl groups on CDs surface generated the CDs-Eu nanocomposite.

The preparation procedure of luminescent ink was described brifely below. 40 mL mixed solution which consisted of water, ethanol and glycerol with volume ratio of 1:3:1 were stirred for an hour. The obtained solution was separated into two parts, one of which was regulated to pH 1.0 using 1 M HCl and the other was adjusted to pH 9.0 with 1 M NaOH. 60 mg CDs-Eu nanocomposite was added into the above solution. Green luminescent ink and invisible ink were obtained after 1 h of ultrasound.

In the pH response experiments, a series of solutions were prepared by adding 50 μL of CDs-Eu solution (2 mg mL−1) into 1950 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solutions with various pH values (1.0–9.0). To investigate the reversibility of CDs-Eu response to pH, CDs-Eu was dissolved in 100 mL buffer solution (pH=1.0). Then the pH of the above mixture was repeatedly adjusted by introducing NaOH or HCl solution. Fluorescence spectra were collected at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm.

In the coding test, the prepared luminescent ink (pH=9.0) and commercial ink were applied as coding and interfering elements to encrypt information. Interestingly, the encrypted information can also be decrypted and re-encrypted with DPA and CuCl2. During the process of anti-counterfeiting, luminescent ink (pH=1.0) was combined with green commercial ink to print bear palms, feet and other anti-counterfeit patterns on filter paper using pen or seal. Part of the patterns can show reversible color changes in HCl and TEA steam.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of the CDs-Eu
ce:section.ce:para: TEM measurements were carried out to characterize the microstructure of CDs and CDs-Eu nanocomposite. As depicted in Fig. 1a and b, both the CDs and CDs-Eu nanocomposite exhibited uniform distribution and high monodispersity. According to the inset, the particle size distribution ranges of CDs and CDs-Eu were concentrated within 1.25–3.25 and 2.55–4.35 nm, and the average particle sizes were about 2.26 and 3.43 nm, respectively. The surface state and chemical composition of CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectra and XPS. Fig. S1 showed the FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-Eu. The absorption peak at 3431 cm−1 was characteristic absorption peak of O-H and N-H [28,29], while the absorption band at 2922 cm−1 corresponded to the C-H stretching vibration of saturated aliphatic hydrocarbons [30]. The peaks at 1640 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of -COOH, which indicated that the surface of CDs was rich in -COOH. Besides, the peak located at 1566 cm−1 was related to the in-plane bending vibration of N-H [31]. In addition, it can be observed that the stretching vibration peak of -COOH shifted to 1540 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra of CDs-Eu, which indicated that the carboxyl group was coordinated with Eu3+ ion. Furthermore, EDS results showed that there were C, N, O and Eu elements in CDs-Eu (Fig. 1c), which proved that the CDs-Eu nanocomposite was successfully prepared.

As depicted in Fig. 1d, in the full scan XPS of CDs-Eu, the peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.8 eV, 532.5 eV and 1134.71 eV were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Eu 3d, respectively, manifesting that the CDs were primarily composed of C, N and O elements, and Eu3+ has been successfully coordinated to the surface of the CDs to obtain CDs-Eu. From the high resolution spectrum of C 1s shown in Fig. 2a, it was found that C mainly existed as CC/C-C (284.5 eV), C-O/C-N (285.5 eV), and CO.

(288.9 eV) [32]. As delineated in Fig. 2b, the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s mainly included two peaks, indicating that N primarily existed in the form of pyrrole nitrogen (401.4 eV) and graphite nitrogen (399.8 eV) [33]. The introduction of nitrogen can effectively regulate the electronic structure of CDs and modify its chemical and physical properties, so as to optimize various properties of CDs. Fig. 2c is the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s. It can be clearly seen that there were three peaks, namely OC (531.3 eV), O-C (532.2 eV) and O-H (533.2 eV) [34]. The XPS characterization results further proved that the surface of CDs contained abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, which was in line with the FT-IR analysis. As portrayed in Fig. 2d, four peaks located at 1164.3, 1157.2, 1142.9 and 1134.7 eV which originated from the multiple splittings of spin orbit of Eu3+ can be clearly observed. Specifically, the four peaks represented Eu3+ 3d5/2, Eu2+ 3d5/2, Eu3+ 3d3/2 and Eu2+ 3d3/2 [35], respectively. The energy difference between two Eu3+ was close to 21.4 eV, which was in line with the value of the coordination energy difference between Eu3+ and O atom [36,37]. The above findings demonstrated that the synthesis of CDs and the functionlization of Eu3+ on the surface of CDs have been successfully achieved.

In this part, we evaluated the fluorescence spectra of the obtained CDs-Eu nanocomposite. The fluorescence emission spectra of the nanocomposite at different excitation wavelengths were demonstrated in Fig. S2. The optimal emission peak located at 525 nm, and strong green light emission can be observed under the UV lamp.

Considering that the protonation degree of functional groups on the surface of CDs may has significant effect on its luminescence intensity [29], which is represented as a response to pH, we investigated the FL response of CDs-Eu to pH. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs-Eu in different pH buffer solutions were recorded. As portrayed in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Eu at 525 nm was significantly enhanced with the increase of pH from 1.0 to 9.0 (λex=280 nm). Meanwhile, the FL color of CDs-Eu solution switched from colorless to green under the UV light radiation centered at a wavelength of 254 nm (Fig. 3b). In the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3c), the luminescence color of CDs-Eu moved from colorless region to green region with the increase of pH, which was consistent with the results of digital luminescence photo of the CDs-Eu solution. The corresponding color coordinate values were provided in Table S1.

Then, the reversibility of the response of CDs-Eu nanocomposite to pH in the pH range of 1.0–9.0 was investigated. The initial pH was regulated to 1.0, then the FL intensity at 525 nm was recorded. Then, 1 M NaOH was added to adjust the pH from 1.0 to 9.0. As depicted in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite enhanced with the increasing of the pH of the solution, and reduced when the pH value decreased to 1.0. The sensing process can be reversible for at least five cycles (Fig. 3d), indicating that CDs-Eu was a promising reversible pH sensor. In addition, the long-term fluorescent stability of CDs-Eu under high pressure xenon lamp irradiation was also investigated. As portrayed in Fig. S3, no obvious intensity variation of CDs-Eu can be observed after 10 h irradiation, manifesting the prominent stability of the nanomaterial.

In view of that surface ligand can be used as an antenna to enhance the luminescence of lanthanide ion, the ligand-sensitized fluorescence emission behavior of the CDs was further investigated. As portrayed in Fig. 4a, with the increase of DPA concentration, the FL intensity increased significantly at 616 nm, while the FL intensity remained basically unchanged at 525 nm. As delineated in Fig. 4b, with the increase of DPA concentration, the CIE coordinates have a transition trend from green to red (the corresponding color coordinates were provided in Table S2), which further verified the color change of the nanocomposite stimulated by different concentrations of DPA, providing the possibility for DPA spray decryption [38].

As depicted in Fig. 4c, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the FL intensity decreased significantly at 616 nm, while the FL intensity remained basically unchanged at 525 nm. The CIE coordinates showed a trend from red to green as the Cu2+ concentration increased (Fig. 4d), and the corresponding color coordinate values were also provided in Table S3. The CIE chromaticity coordinates further verified the color change of the nanocomposite under the stimulation of Cu2+, which provided a possibility for Cu2+ spray encryption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs-Eu to pH, DPA and subsequent Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To study the interaction mechanism between pH and CDs-Eu, the fluorescence lifetime, zeta potential and TEM images of CDs-Eu under different pH conditions were collected. As portrayed in Fig. S4, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs in CDs-Eu decreased from 1.248 ns to 0.592 ns when pH were regulated from 9.0 to 1.0, which restored to 1.253 ns when the pH was readjusted to 9.0 (the corresponding photophysical data were shown in Table S4). This phenomenon indicated that the CDs-Eu had intense interaction with protons.

In addition, the zeta potential values of the CDs-Eu at different pH were measured to investigate its surface states. As depicted in Fig. S5, the zeta potentials of the CDs-Eu at pH 9.0, 5.0 and 1.0 were − 28.1, − 16.9 and − 3.58 mV, respectively, which were in agreement with negatively charged -COO- groups on the surface of CDs-Eu. The high charge repulsion force at high pH condition facilitated the dispersion of the CDs-Eu [29]. As delineated in Fig. S6, the CDs-Eu exhibited monodispersity under pH 6.0 while aggregation occurred when the pH decreased to 1.0. The above results indicated that the pH responsive fluorescence signal was originated from the aggregation and disaggregation of CDs-Eu under different pH conditions.

As a well known sensitizer for lanthanide ions (Ln3+), DPA can coordinate to Ln3+ and transfer its absorbed energy to Ln3+ effectively to enhance the fluorescence of Ln3+ [39–42]. To investigate the interaction mechanism between DPA and CDs-Eu, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs-Eu nanocomposite in the absence and presence of DPA were measured. As depicted in Fig. S7, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+ in CDs-Eu significantly increased from 0.722 ms to 1.129 ms in the presence of DPA, manifesting that DPA can transfer its absorbed energy to Eu3+ effectively. The corresponding photophysical data were shown in Table S5.

To investigate the interaction mechanism between Cu2+ and CDs-Eu-DPA, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. As depicted in Fig. S8, the CDs-Eu-DPA composite exhibited two absorbance bands at 270 and 278 nm, which was due to the π–π * transitions of the bound DPA. After introducing Cu2+ ions, the peak at 270 nm shifted to 268 nm and the peak at 278 nm diminished. The above spectra was similar to that of the Cu-DPA complex, manifesting the interaction between Cu2+ and DPA, which broke the coordination bond between DPA and Eu3+ ions, inhibiting the energy transfer from DPA to Eu3+ ions [43].

Considering the excellent fluorescence characteristics of CDs-Eu, we combined it with the rules of Morse code to extend its potential application to the field of data security [44,45]. Firstly, in order to ensure that data was not easily leaked, we use invisible fluorescent ink with pH= 1.0 as the basis for encryption and stability. As shown in Figs. 5a, b, in the first encryption process, the Morse code of the word "COMPUTER" was handwritten with invisible fluorescent ink (CDs-Eu solution at pH 1.0), and the encrypted information was invisible under both the natural light and UV light. During the decryption step, as demonstrated in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence color of the encrypted word "COMPUTER" turned to green when encountered the TEA steam. Then the key information can be clearly read according to the rules of the Morse code. Due to the prominent pH reversibility of the CDs-Eu, the confidential information can be re-encrypted into a blank paper by placing it in HCl vapor after reading it (Fig. 5d). This demonstrated high security in information dissemination, and people can read and hide the information timely [46].

Since the outstanding pH reversibility and DPA responsiveness of CDs-Eu, the synthesized nanocomposite was also applicable to anti-counterfeiting. As portrayed in Figs. 6a, b, when the invisible ink with pH= 1.0 was coated on the pattern, the information was invisible under both the sunlight (Fig. 6a1, 6b1) and UV light (Fig. 6a2, 6b2). After treatment with TEA vapor, the pattern was still invisible under sunlight (Fig. 6a3, b3), but could be seen immediately under 254 nm UV light (Fig. 6a4, b4), so as to realize multi-level information encryption.

In addition, thanks to the effective sensitization of the lanthanide europium ion by DPA, the multi-color emission pattern was obtained, and the information encryption and deecryption was also achieved. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the pattern consisted of two parts, commercial green ink and green fluorescent ink made by the nanocomposite (pH=9.0) were combined to form 8 birds. The pattern can not be seen under natural light (Fig. 6c1), while the overall pattern showed green fluorescence under 254 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6c2). After spraying the DPA, the fluorescent color of the birds drawn with the nanocomposite turned to red, while those drawn with green commercial ink remained unchanged (Fig. 6c3). Interestingly, the birds that have turned to red can return to the original green color after spraying with Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 6c4. As depicted in Fig. 6d, the toxic hazard sign was drawn by green fluorescent ink made from the nanocomposite (pH=9.0). After spraying the DPA, the pattern turned to red, and then returned to green after spraying Cu2+. These fascinating luminescence phenomena indicated that the luminescence of anti-counterfeiting labels made by CDs-Eu nanocomposite can be modulated by acid-base, DPA and Cu2+ stimulation, thus increasing the forge difficulty. Compared with the reported work, the synthesized nanomaterial can significantly enhance the secrecy and security of information transmission.

In summary, a facile and effective way was presented to construct multistimuli intelligent luminescent nanomaterial based on europium doped carbon dots (CDs-Eu) for advanced information encryption. This CDs-Eu nanocomposite exhibited controllable color changes upon exposure to acid-base, DPA and Cu2+, respectively, manifesting an enormous potential in multilevel encryption application. Combined with Morse code and anti-counterfeiting pattern, the encryption, decryption and anti-counterfeiting of important information can be realized. This work is conducive to the exploration of low cost and highly effective materials for advanced and secure information encryption.

Lina Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Feng Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Junyu Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yongxin Li: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071, U1904168, and 51773052), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (No. 222300420047), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No. 222102320446), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (NSFRF210331).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.130043.

Supplementary material
.

Matter
